text: The Stoics used the metaphor of a citadel to describe the inner life. Marcus Aurelius writes that the rational mind is 'a fortress to which a person can retreat at any time and find peace.' But a fortress is only useful if its gates are monitored. Prosoche was the gatekeeping function: attention directed inward, scrutinizing which impressions were being allowed to enter and shape judgment. Unlike modern mindfulness, which often aims at non-judgmental observation, Stoic vigilance was explicitly evaluative — each incoming impression was to be assessed immediately against reason. The citadel metaphor captures this dual role: protection and discernment, not mere awareness.
question: How does the passage distinguish Stoic prosoche from modern mindfulness?

options.text: Prosoche focuses on breath awareness while mindfulness focuses on thought content
Prosoche is practiced only in the evening while mindfulness is continuous
Prosoche is explicitly evaluative, assessing impressions against reason, while mindfulness aims at non-judgmental observation
Prosoche addresses external events while mindfulness addresses internal states
